mappings.rationale: Covers the rise of Russia from Ivan IV through Catherine the Great, including westernization, the Time of Troubles, and expansion (A.3).
Covers Habsburg rivalry with the Ottomans, Austrian conquest of Hungary at Mohacs, and the Austro-Turkish wars (A.2).
Covers the Ottoman Empire's Islamic governance, classical Ottoman society, and religious and civil law (B.1.a.ii).
The Ottoman Empire as the dominant Islamic power and the Safavid dynasty in Iran are central episodes in Islamic history (B.1-B.2).
The Safavid dynasty, conflict with the Turks, Abbas I's restoration, Nadir Shah, and the Qajar dynasty are explicitly covered in B.2.a.
The Ottoman, Russian, and Safavid empires and their expansions and conflicts are major imperial systems covered throughout this section.
The impact of the Enlightenment on education and social change in 18th-century Russia is explicitly discussed in A.3.e.
The emergence of national consciousness among Poles, Hungarians, and peoples of the Ottoman and Russian empires is relevant to A.1-A.2.
The Ottoman conquest of Constantinople and the end of the Byzantine Empire provide the starting point for the Ottoman state discussed in B.1.
The intellectual life of the Ottoman and Safavid empires involved continuing traditions of Islamic philosophy (B.1-B.2).
The Counter-Reformation's effects on Poland-Lithuania and Hungary, including the spread of Protestantism and Catholic reaction, are covered in A.1-A.2.
The Thirty Years' War's impact on eastern European states and Habsburg-Ottoman rivalry intersect with Holy Roman Empire history (A.1-A.2).
Classical Ottoman society's religious and civil law system is explicitly discussed in B.1.a.ii.
The Ottoman and Safavid empires as major Islamic powers represent Islam's global political and cultural reach in the early modern period (B.1-B.2).
Ottoman and Safavid control of trade routes and Nadir Shah's invasions of India and Turkistan connect to the Silk Road's legacy (B.2.a.ii).
